content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
November 13, 1991

Stella Matthews, Head
Campaign Reporting Section
Los Angeles County Registrar-Recorder
P. O. Box 30450
Los Angeles, CA  90030

Re: Your Request for Confirmation
of Telephone Advice
Our File No. A-91-478

Dear Ms. Matthews:

You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided to you concerning the campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/  Specifically, you asked a series of questions concerning the campaign disclosure obligations of two controlled committees formed in July 1991 to oppose two recall measures.

A summary of Ms. Hughes' advice, including the clarifying advice she provided after receipt of your letter, is outlined below.

1.  Committees formed to support or oppose the qualification of a ballot measure are required to file the 21-day post-qualification campaign statement specified in Section 84200.5(f). Therefore, Taxpayers Against Recall and Citizens Against Recall Effort (the "committees") were required to file the 21-day statement only if they received contributions or made expenditures for the purpose of opposing the qualification of the measures.

Although the committees' Statements of Organization (Form 410) do not indicate that they were formed to oppose qualification of the measures and you have indicated that you have no information about the committees' expenditures, we recommend that you notify the committees of their potential obligation to file the 21-day post-qualification statement.

________________________

1/  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Stella Matthews
Our File No. A-91-478
Page 2


     2.     All recipient committees are required to file semi-
annual campaign statements each year and, when the candidate
controlling a committee or the measure for which a committee is
formed is being voted upon, pre-election campaign statements must
be filed. (Sections 84200, 84200.5-84200.7.) If the committees
in question were formed only for the purpose of opposing the
recall measures and not to oppose qualification of the measures,
the deadline for their first campaign statement will depend on the
date, if any, of the recall elections.

     If the elections are held during 1991 or on or before
February 14, 1992, the committees' first filing obligation will be
the pre-election campaign statement due 40 days before the
election. The period covered will be January 1, 1991, through 45
days before the election.2/  (Section 84200.8.) If the elections
are held after February 14, 1992, the committees' first filing
obligation will be the semi-annual statement due January 31, 1992,
covering the period January 1, 1991 through December 31, 1991, and
the committees will file first and second pre-election campaign
statements in 1992 as specified in Section 84200.5. After the
elections, the committees must continue filing semi-annual
campaign statements until they are eligible to terminate and file
Form 415. (Sections 82013, 84200, 84214; Regulation 18404.)

     If the recall effort does not qualify for the ballot, the
committees will file semi-annual statements until they are
eligible to terminate and file Form 415. (Sections 82013, 84200,
84214; Regulation 18404.)

     If you have additional questions, please contact me at (916)
322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        By:  Carla Wardlow
                                             Assistant Division Chief
                                             Technical Assistance &
                                                 Analysis Division


_________________
2/     For example, if a recall election is scheduled for
February 4, 1992, the first pre-election statement will cover the
period January 1, 1991, through December 21, 1991, and will be due
on December 26, 1991.

sections.question: Concerning the campaign disclosure obligations of two controlled committees formed in July 1991 to oppose two recall measures, specifically regarding: (1) whether committees formed to oppose qualification of a ballot measure must file the 21-day post-qualification campaign statement; (2) what filing obligations apply to recipient committees for semi-annual and pre-election campaign statements; and (3) how filing deadlines are determined based on recall election dates.
sections.conclusion: Committees formed to support or oppose qualification of a ballot measure must file the 21-day post-qualification campaign statement only if they received contributions or made expenditures for opposing qualification. All recipient committees must file semi-annual statements and pre-election statements when applicable. Filing deadlines depend on recall election dates: if held during 1991 or by February 14, 1992, the first filing is a pre-election statement due 40 days before election; if after February 14, 1992, the first filing is the semi-annual statement due January 31, 1992. Committees must continue filing until eligible to terminate.

sections.question_synthetic: What are the campaign disclosure filing obligations for two committees formed in July 1991 to oppose recall measures, including whether they must file 21-day post-qualification statements and what semi-annual and pre-election statement deadlines apply?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Committees formed to oppose recall measures must file 21-day post-qualification statements only if they spent money opposing qualification. Filing obligations for semi-annual and pre-election statements depend on whether recall elections occur before or after February 14, 1992, with different deadlines for each scenario. Committees must continue filing semi-annual statements until they are eligible to terminate.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR-scanned document with minor formatting irregularities. Question reconstructed from context as letter explicitly confirms prior telephone advice. Document is confirmation letter referencing earlier telephone advice from Ms. Hughes. All key sections present and clearly identified.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Concerning the campaign disclosure obligations of two controlled committees formed in July 1991 to oppose two recall measures, specifically regarding: (1) whether committees formed to oppose qualification of a ballot measure must file the 21-day post-qualification campaign statement; (2) what filing obligations apply to recipient committees for semi-annual and pre-election campaign statements; and (3) how filing deadlines are determined based on recall election dates.

CONCLUSION: Committees formed to support or oppose qualification of a ballot measure must file the 21-day post-qualification campaign statement only if they received contributions or made expenditures for opposing qualification. All recipient committees must file semi-annual statements and pre-election statements when applicable. Filing deadlines depend on recall election dates: if held during 1991 or by February 14, 1992, the first filing is a pre-election statement due 40 days before election; if after February 14, 1992, the first filing is the semi-annual statement due January 31, 1992. Committees must continue filing until eligible to terminate.

embedding.summary: The FPPC confirms telephone advice regarding campaign disclosure obligations for two committees formed to oppose recall measures in July 1991, clarifying requirements for 21-day post-qualification statements and establishing filing deadlines based on recall election dates.
